text: Tensor Memory (TMEM) is a dedicated 256KB on-chip memory per SM for Tensor Core operations. Structured as 512 columns x 128 lanes of 32-bit cells. Uses lane-column addressing. Achieves 420 clock cycle end-to-end access (58% reduction vs Hopper's 1000-cycle global memory). Provides 16 TB/s read bandwidth and 8 TB/s write bandwidth per SM, operating additively with L1/SMEM bandwidth.
relevance: TMEM is key differentiator for Blackwell MoE kernels — keeps accumulators and intermediates on-chip

text: TMEM optimal efficiency at 64x64 element tiles (4KB for FP8), aligning with 1024-bit memory interface. This is a departure from Hopper's 32x32 optimal tile size. Tiles <32x32 achieve only 45% of peak bandwidth. Tiles >128x128 trigger multi-phase transfers reducing throughput by 30%.
relevance: Directly informs BLOCK_M and BLOCK_N choices for Triton kernels targeting B200

text: TMEM operates with independent address generation units that pre-fetch tensor tiles into staging buffers, enabling tcgen05.mma to overlap data movement with computation. While a warp group executes tensor operations on one TMEM tile, copy engines asynchronously populate the next, achieving near-perfect double buffering.
relevance: Key for achieving peak throughput in MoE GEMM — pipeline must overlap TMA loads with MMA compute

text: For chained matrix multiplications D=(A×B)×C, keeping intermediate result in TMEM eliminates an estimated 12 TB of data movement per second per fully utilized SM compared to Hopper's write-back-to-global approach. TMEM should be prioritized for multi-stage tensor pipelines with large working sets. For small matrices fitting in L1, forcing TMEM introduces 3-5% overhead.
relevance: MoE has chained GEMM1 → activation → GEMM2; keeping intermediate in TMEM is critical optimization

text: tcgen05.mma achieves 2.9-11.6x lower single-instruction latency than Hopper wgmma. Latency is nearly constant across tile sizes (11.0-11.4 cycles), while Hopper scales linearly. This confirms spatial array design rather than temporal pipelining. Warp-level granularity (32 threads) reduces scheduler stalls by 18-23% in memory-bound kernels vs Hopper's warp-group (128 threads).
relevance: Memory bandwidth is the bottleneck, not tensor core latency — optimize for memory access patterns

text: FP8: 3851 TFLOPS (96.3% peak). FP4: 7702 TFLOPS (96.3% peak). INT8: 3927 TOPS (slightly exceeds FP8). FP16 with FP32 accum halves throughput (1929 vs 964 TFLOPS). Despite 177x throughput difference between FP64 (44.8) and FP4 (7702), latency varies only 1.27x. Throughput scaling via wider datapaths, not deeper pipelining.
relevance: FP32 accumulator costs 50% throughput — use FP16 accumulator when possible for MoE inference

text: B200 is dual-die: 208 billion transistors, 148 SMs across 8 GPCs, 4 L2 partitions (double Hopper). Two dies unified by NV-HBI providing coherent single-device view. 192 GB HBM3e unified memory space. This architecture emphasizes post-training and inference efficiency over raw training FLOPS.
relevance: Understanding physical topology helps with expert-to-SM mapping and NUMA-aware scheduling

text: Blackwell replaces warp-synchronous wgmma with tcgen05.mma — a single-thread instruction. Each thread independently issues MMA operations, removing warp-level synchronization. Operands from SMEM and TMEM (new pathway). This reduces idle cycles and enables per-thread scheduling for tensor operations.
relevance: tcgen05 instructions are not directly accessible from Triton — CUTLASS/CuTeDSL needed for direct TMEM control

text: Blackwell native FP4 (e2m1) and FP6 (e3m2) support. FP4: MXFP4 (block-32 with E8M0 scale) and NVFP4 (block-16 with e4m3 scale). Hardware dequantization converts FP4→FP8/FP16 during MMA. FP6 provides 1.33x bandwidth savings vs FP8 with better dynamic range.
relevance: Future optimization path: FP4 weights could double throughput to 7702 TFLOPS if accuracy permits

text: Tensor cores achieve 96-99% of peak across all precisions — they are NOT the bottleneck. Memory bandwidth and kernel launch overhead are the limiting factors. This means MoE kernel optimization should focus on: (1) memory access patterns, (2) L2 cache utilization, (3) reducing kernel launches, (4) TMA/TMEM pipeline efficiency.
relevance: Critical insight: do not optimize compute, optimize memory — this changes the entire optimization strategy

text: Blackwell supports CTA pair execution: two CTAs with adjacent ranks share operands via dedicated intra-TPC communication network. Reduces redundant data movement. Maps to 2-SM MMA in CUTLASS. Each CTA pair shares a TPC, enabling efficient operand sharing without going through global memory.
relevance: Use 2-SM MMA mode (CUTLASS MMA2SMConfig) for large tiles to leverage CTA pair communication

text: Blackwell has a hardware Decompression Engine (DE) that offloads decompression from SMs. Supports various algorithms for transparent decompression during memory access. Model weights can be stored compressed in HBM3e and decompressed on access.
relevance: Future path: compressed weight storage could reduce HBM bandwidth requirements for MoE expert weights
